pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: Before admission, Ms. H started a new diet pill containing Phenylpropanolamine, and the IV medication given for agitation on Day 4 was Midazolam. Both are potent cytochrome P450 inducers/metabolized compounds known to precipitate AIP crises. Subsequent 24-hour urine collection confirmed drastically elevated Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is an inherited metabolic disorder resulting from a partial deficiency of the hepatic enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS). When triggered by specific drugs (CYP450 inducers, sulfonamides, barbiturates), fasting, stress, or hormones, the synthesis of heme is disrupted. This leads to the buildup of neurotoxic precursors—specifically ALA and PBG—in the liver and circulating plasma. The clinical presentation is entirely neurological, as ALA is structurally similar to GABA and can cross the blood-brain barrier. 1. Abdominal Pain: Severe, diffuse, often localized to the mid-abdomen, caused by autonomic neuropathy in the GI tract. 2. Autonomic Dysfunction: Severe hypertension and tachycardia due to central sympathetic overdrive. Hyponatremia (123 mEq/L) is often caused by Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH), also linked to autonomic disruption. 3. Neuropathy: The ascending motor paralysis mimics Guillain-Barré Syndrome but usually spares sensory function. This polyneuropathy is demyelinating and axonal, often leading to respiratory paralysis. 4. Psychiatric Symptoms: Due to direct neurotoxicity in the CNS. Treatment involves high-dose IV Heme Arginate (e.g., Panhematin) to downregulate the heme synthesis pathway, halting the production of toxic precursors.
